title: Helium: an element found in the Sun twenty-seven years before it was caught on Earth
intro: On 18 August 1868 a total solar eclipse swept the Indian Ocean. Pierre Janssen, observing from Guntur on the Bay of Bengal, aimed a spectroscope (a slit, a prism, an eyepiece, the new instrument that decomposed light into its component wavelengths) at the chromosphere (the thin red shell of solar atmosphere that flares up at totality) and saw a yellow emission line where no terrestrial element placed one. Three months later Norman Lockyer in London, working without an eclipse from a window in Hampstead, found the same line and proposed naming the substance helium, after Helios, the Sun. The element existed on no chemist's shelf and resisted every laboratory search for a generation; the line stayed an embarrassment until William Ramsay, in 1895, heated a uranium mineral in acid and saw the yellow line in his own laboratory at last.

sections.heading: 1. The eclipse at Guntur and the slit at the limb

blocks.text: The 18 August 1868 eclipse track ran from the Persian Gulf across India and into Indochina. Janssen, a French astronomer who had been chasing eclipses for the better part of a decade, set up at Guntur on the Bay of Bengal with a spectroscope of his own design: an entrance slit, a small flint-glass prism, and a viewing eyepiece. Eclipse spectroscopy was less than a year old; Bunsen and Kirchhoff had founded the technique in Heidelberg in 1859, identifying terrestrial elements by the wavelengths of their emission lines. Aiming a Bunsen-and-Kirchhoff spectroscope at the chromosphere had never been done. Janssen pointed his slit at the bright red rim of solar atmosphere that flared into view when the moon covered the photosphere (the visible disk of the Sun, 6000 K, the source of the broadband Fraunhofer absorption spectrum) and read off the lines.

What he saw, on a coal-black background, was a handful of bright emission lines: hydrogen Hα at 656.3 nm, several other hydrogen lines, and a yellow line he initially took for the sodium D-doublet. The brightness of the chromospheric emission against the dark eclipse sky was so high that Janssen realized he could observe the same lines the next day, eclipse over, by aligning his slit tangent to the bright limb of the uneclipsed Sun. The sky background outside the limb is dim; the chromosphere is bright; the contrast is enough. He wired Paris with the news (his telegram took six weeks to reach the Académie des Sciences) and stayed at Guntur for two months observing solar prominences (the looped magnetic structures that flare up out of the chromosphere) in broad daylight.

Lockyer, in London, had been chasing the same trick from the opposite direction. He had built a high-dispersion spectroscope, paid for partly out of his own pocket, with the explicit goal of reading chromospheric emission lines without waiting for an eclipse. He succeeded in October 1868. He compared his yellow line carefully to the sodium D-doublet (the pair of bright absorption lines D₁ at 589.59 nm and D₂ at 589.00 nm, the spectroscopic signature of sodium that Bunsen used as a calibration standard in every flame test in Europe) and saw that the chromospheric line sat at 587.49 nm, a clean 1.5 nm to the violet of D₂. The letters of Janssen and Lockyer reached the Académie on the same day, 26 October 1868. The Académie struck a commemorative medal with the two men's profiles on it.

sections.heading: 2. Why a yellow line at 587.49 nm had to be a new element

blocks.text: The case for a new element was a matter of careful wavelength bookkeeping. Sodium gives a yellow doublet whose origin had been understood since the 1850s: the 3p → 3s transition of the valence electron, split into two closely spaced lines by spin-orbit coupling (the small magnetic interaction between the electron's orbital motion and its own intrinsic spin, which separates the 3p level into 3p₁/₂ and 3p₃/₂). D₁ at 589.59 nm and D₂ at 589.00 nm differ by only 0.59 nm, and to the eye the doublet looks like a single yellow line. The chromospheric line sat farther away. The wavelength labeling continued the German Fraunhofer notation (A through K, marking the strongest dark lines in the solar absorption spectrum), and the new line was provisionally called D₃, slotting in among the established D-lines even though it was an emission line rather than an absorption line.

Translate D₃'s wavelength into a photon energy using the Planck relation. λ is the wavelength of the line. h is Planck's constant. c is the speed of light in vacuum. The product hc converts wavelength to energy, with hc ≈ 1240 eV·nm a useful shortcut: divide by the wavelength in nanometers and read off the energy in electron-volts.

blocks.caption: E_D3 is the photon energy of the D₃ emission line. h is Planck's constant. c is the speed of light in vacuum. λ_D3 is the wavelength of the line, 587.49 nm. The product hc ≈ 1240 eV·nm is a convenient form for visible photons. For D₃ at 587.49 nm the photon energy is 2.11 eV, comfortably in the visible band and easily emitted by an atomic transition in the few-eV range.

blocks.text: Lockyer and the British chemist Edward Frankland (a co-author on the helium papers and the founder of the Institute of Chemistry) checked the 587.49 nm line against every flame, arc, and spark spectrum they could ignite in their laboratory at the Royal College of Chemistry. Sodium was excluded by the 1.5 nm offset, iron was excluded by the absence of accompanying iron lines, calcium gave no yellow line near that wavelength, and a long list of other candidates failed the same way. They concluded the line came from an element that simply was not present, or at any rate not in observable quantity, anywhere on the Earth's surface. Lockyer coined the name helium from ἥλιος, helios, the Sun, in 1870. To name an element from a single line in the chromosphere, with no terrestrial sample, no atomic weight, no chemical behavior, was outside the rules. Dmitri Mendeleev had just laid out the periodic table in 1869; helium did not fit any of his rows, and most chemists outside Lockyer's circle treated the proposal as premature speculation for two decades.

quiz.question: What was the strongest 1868 argument that the chromospheric yellow line at 587.49 nm was a new element, not a known terrestrial one observed under unusual conditions?
options: The line was emitted from the chromosphere rather than absorbed in the photosphere, which proved by itself that no known element could produce it.

Its wavelength sat 1.5 nm to the violet of the sodium D₂ line at 589.00 nm, well outside the 0.59 nm sodium D-doublet and far from the wavelengths of every other element catalogued in the 1860s flame, arc, and spark spectra.
The line was brighter than the hydrogen Hα line at 656.3 nm in Janssen's chromospheric spectrum, and the brightness alone identifies a new element.
Lockyer's spectroscope showed that the line had a different polarization than the sodium D-lines, which can only be explained by a different electronic transition in a different atom.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 inverts the physics: emission and absorption lines of the same element sit at the same wavelengths (Kirchhoff's law, the 1859 result that founded astronomical spectroscopy), so the chromospheric line being an emission line does not by itself rule out a known element. The line's identification rested on its wavelength, not its emission/absorption character. Option 2 conflates brightness with identification; the relative intensity of two lines depends on excitation conditions in the chromosphere and on the oscillator strengths of the transitions, neither of which fingerprints an element. Option 3 invents a polarization measurement Lockyer never made; spectroscopic polarimetry in 1868 was not at the precision required, and the line's polarization in the chromosphere is in fact dominated by the magnetic field of the prominence, not by anything atomic. Option 1 is the honest answer. The argument was wavelength bookkeeping. Sodium D₁ at 589.59 nm and D₂ at 589.00 nm are split by 0.59 nm by spin-orbit coupling of the 3p state; the chromospheric line at 587.49 nm sat 1.5 nm to the violet of D₂, three times the doublet's own splitting. Every other element catalogued in the 1860s flame-test and arc spectra (iron, calcium, magnesium, strontium, the alkali metals, the alkaline earths) was checked at the Royal College of Chemistry and excluded by the absence of matching companion lines at expected positions. The line had no terrestrial counterpart in any laboratory spectrum, and that absence, line by line and element by element, was the case for a new element.

sections.heading: 3. Reading the D-region: how a spectroscope's resolving power decides what counts as one line or three

blocks.text: Whether D₁, D₂, and D₃ look like one yellow blob or three sharp lines is set by the resolving power of the instrument. The resolving power R = λ/Δλ of a spectroscope (the ratio of the wavelength being observed to the smallest wavelength interval it can separate as two distinct lines, the figure of merit Lord Rayleigh formalized in 1879) decides whether the 0.59 nm sodium doublet is even visible as a doublet, and whether the 1.5 nm gap between D₂ and D₃ stands out clearly enough to argue for a new element. Janssen's 1868 spectroscope had R near 500; the doublet blurred into a single yellow line and D₃ was a slightly displaced bump. Lockyer's October 1868 spectroscope had R near 4000; the sodium doublet split cleanly and D₃ stood as a distinct line of its own. The argument for a new element required Lockyer's instrument, not Janssen's.

The instrument geometry mattered as much as the resolving power. Both Janssen and Lockyer worked with prism spectroscopes (the diffraction grating, ruled by Fraunhofer in 1821 and developed into a practical instrument by Henry Rowland after 1882, was not yet the standard astronomical tool). The slit was aligned tangent to the solar limb, picking off a thin sliver of chromosphere bright enough to read against the dim sky outside the photosphere. The prism dispersed the light into a horizontal rainbow; the eyepiece scanned along it; the observer drew the lines by hand against a comparison spectrum produced by a laboratory lamp shining through a second slit alongside the solar one. Lockyer used a sodium discharge lamp as his wavelength reference, which is why the sodium D-lines play the role of a ruler in every plate he published.

The wavelength scale settled in the following years. Anders Ångström, working at Uppsala, published an atlas of the solar spectrum in 1868 with wavelengths in units of 10⁻¹⁰ m (the unit that would carry his name from 1907). Helium's D₃ landed at 5876 Å, the same 587.6 nm to within a hundredth of a nanometer of the modern measurement. The modern value is set by spectroscopy of the neutral helium triplet system: the 587.56 nm line corresponds to the transition between the 3³D and 2³P states of the helium atom, where the superscript 3 marks the triplet (the two electrons of helium having parallel spins, S = 1, in contrast to the singlet states where they are antiparallel, S = 0). Lockyer of course knew none of this in 1868. The triplet/singlet electronic structure of helium was not understood until quantum mechanics in the 1920s. What Lockyer had was a wavelength to four significant figures, and that was enough.

blocks.caption: The yellow D-region of the solar emission spectrum at variable spectroscopic resolution. The slider sweeps the resolving power R = λ/Δλ from R = 200 (Janssen's modest 1868 traveling spectroscope, where D₁, D₂, and D₃ blur into a single yellow bump) up to R = 12,000 (a Rowland-class concave grating from the 1890s, where every line stands alone). The three lines plotted are sodium D₁ at 589.59 nm, sodium D₂ at 589.00 nm, and helium D₃ at 587.49 nm. Drag the slider slowly and watch D₃ peel away from the sodium doublet around R = 800, then watch the doublet itself split around R = 1500. The historical marker at R = 4000 is roughly where Lockyer's October 1868 spectroscope sat, the resolving power that made the case for helium.

sections.heading: 4. Twenty-seven years, then a uranium mineral and a discharge lamp

blocks.text: Between 1868 and 1895 helium had the awkward status of an element that lived in the Sun and refused to come down. The naming had been provocative, and the wait for an Earth-bound confirmation became long enough that several chemists publicly doubted it. Through the 1870s Lockyer continued chromospheric work, identifying additional lines in the corona that he tentatively attributed to other new elements (most of these were later identified as highly ionized iron and nickel, not new elements; one of the worst false trails was 'coronium', the green coronal line later resolved as Fe XIV, fourteen-times-ionized iron, by Bengt Edlén in 1939). The boy who cried 'new element' became gradually less believed, and helium's status suffered by association.

The breakthrough came not from astronomy but from terrestrial gas chemistry. William Ramsay, professor at University College London, was systematically isolating the noble gases (the column of chemically inert elements that Mendeleev's periodic table had not predicted because no example was known in 1869). With Lord Rayleigh he had isolated argon from the atmosphere in 1894, by removing every other component of air and weighing what remained. The work had given him a battery of techniques (mercury pumps, calcium drying, hot copper to absorb residual oxygen, spectroscopic readout of the purified residual gas) for getting a chemically pure trace gas out of a mixed sample.

In late March 1895 Ramsay came across a remark in a paper by the American chemist William Hillebrand. Hillebrand had heated cleveite (a variety of the uranium mineral uraninite, named after the Swedish chemist Per Teodor Cleve) in dilute sulfuric acid in 1888 and collected an unreactive gas that he had assumed was atmospheric nitrogen. Ramsay tried the same. He took a sample of cleveite, heated it in acid, captured the released gas in an evacuated tube, fed the gas into a discharge tube, and ran an electric current through it. The tube glowed yellow. He looked at the glow through a spectroscope. The D₃ line was there.

Ramsay sent samples to William Crookes (London's leading spectroscopist) and to Cleve and Karl Theodor Langlet in Sweden, who confirmed within weeks. By April 1895, twenty-seven years after Janssen's chromospheric line, helium had a terrestrial source and a sealed glass vial of pure gas to go with it. The mechanism of its presence in cleveite was not yet known, but the next decade would settle it: helium-4 nuclei are the alpha particles emitted by uranium and thorium and their decay chains, and cleveite ores are saturated with helium that has been trickling out of radioactive decays for hundreds of millions of years. The connection between solar chromosphere and uranium ore was no coincidence.

blocks.caption: ²³⁸U is uranium-238, the most abundant isotope of uranium. ²³⁴Th is thorium-234, the daughter nucleus left behind after one alpha decay. α is the alpha particle ejected by the decay. ⁴He²⁺ is a doubly-ionized helium-4 atom, the bare nucleus consisting of two protons and two neutrons. The identity α ≡ ⁴He²⁺ is what makes uranium ores reservoirs of helium: every alpha decay releases one helium nucleus into the mineral matrix, where it captures two electrons over geological time and is released when the ore is dissolved in acid.

quiz.question: Why did the helium in Ramsay's 1895 cleveite experiment come out as a chemically inert gas trapped inside a uranium-bearing mineral, rather than as a chemically combined helium compound that had to be reduced?
options: Cleveite contains a helium-bearing oxide HeO that decomposes thermally when heated in acid, releasing free helium; the inertness is a property of the released gas but not of the helium in the mineral.

Helium is chemically inert in its ground state because its 1s² electronic configuration is a closed shell, with the next available orbital (2s) well above the first ionization energy at 24.6 eV. It forms no terrestrial compound under ordinary conditions. The helium in cleveite is therefore not bonded chemically but trapped physically, trickled in over geological time as alpha particles ejected by the radioactive decay of uranium and thorium, captured two electrons each, and accumulated in the crystal lattice. Acid dissolution breaks the lattice; the gas escapes.
The helium in cleveite is bonded to uranium as a helide complex UHe₆ that breaks apart only in strongly acidic solution, which is why earlier heating experiments without acid failed to release it.

Helium in cleveite is in fact chemically combined with the oxygen of the uranate group; heating in acid protonates the oxygen and frees the helium, exactly as carbonates release carbon dioxide under acid attack.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 invents a helium oxide HeO that does not exist; no stable terrestrial chemical compound of helium has ever been isolated under ordinary laboratory conditions, and the inertness of helium is a consequence of its closed-shell electronic structure, not of any decomposition reaction. Option 2 invents a UHe₆ helide complex which is also fictional; uranium forms no such compound with helium and there is no chemistry of this kind to invoke. Option 3 misreads the chemistry of the cleveite reaction; the acid dissolves the uranate to release uranyl ions and the released gas is helium that was trapped in the mineral, not helium that was chemically bonded to the oxygen. The honest answer is option 1. Helium has the electron configuration 1s², a closed shell, and its first ionization energy (24.6 eV, the highest of any neutral atom) means there is no available orbital to share or accept in any normal chemical bond. Helium is the cleanest example of an inert gas in the periodic table. Its presence in cleveite is therefore not chemical but physical: each alpha decay of uranium-238 or thorium-232 (or any of the intermediate daughters in their decay chains) emits a helium-4 nucleus into the surrounding mineral lattice. The nuclei come to rest within tens of microns of their parent atoms, pick up two electrons from the surroundings to become neutral helium atoms, and remain trapped in the crystal interstitial spaces. Ramsay's acid dissolution of the cleveite breaks the lattice and releases the trapped helium as a free gas. This mechanism was confirmed in the early 1900s by Robert Strutt's measurements of helium accumulation rates in uranium-bearing rocks, the same measurements that would later be adapted by Ernest Rutherford and Frederick Soddy into one of the first geological dating methods (uranium-helium dating, the predecessor of uranium-lead dating). The link between Lockyer's chromospheric line and Ramsay's cleveite gas is therefore a chain that runs from atomic physics (the 1s² closed shell) through nuclear physics (alpha decay) through geochemistry (mineral retention of helium) to chemistry (acid dissolution), and every link in the chain was forged in the forty years following the discovery.

sections.heading: 5. Manchester, 1908: a discharge tube closes the loop

blocks.text: The story did not end with Ramsay's vial of pure gas. The deepest question was still open: what is the alpha particle, and why does a uranium ore release helium? Ernest Rutherford, working in Montreal and then Manchester, spent the decade after 1898 fingerprinting the radiations of radium. He had separated the emissions into alpha (the heaviest and least penetrating, stopped by a sheet of paper), beta (lighter, stopped by a few millimeters of aluminum), and gamma (electromagnetic, unstoppable by normal matter). By 1903 he had deflected alpha rays in electric and magnetic fields, measured their charge-to-mass ratio, and tentatively identified the alpha as a doubly-ionized helium atom, ⁴He²⁺. The identification was indirect: q/m came out near 4.8 × 10⁷ C/kg, which matched ⁴He²⁺ exactly, but matching a ratio is not the same as catching the element itself.

The direct test came in 1908. Rutherford, by then at Manchester, designed an apparatus with Thomas Royds that asked the alpha particle, in effect, to identify itself spectroscopically. They blew a glass tube with an extraordinarily thin inner wall, no thicker than 0.01 mm, the smallest dimension Manchester's glassblowers could deliver. Inside the inner tube they sealed a small sample of radon, the radioactive gas Rutherford had identified as a daughter product of radium. Radon emits alpha particles at known energies (5.5 MeV), and the inner-tube wall was just thin enough to let alpha particles pass through while keeping the radon gas itself inside. Around the inner tube they evacuated an outer tube, capped with electrodes at the top for a discharge.

Then they waited six days. Over that time alpha particles ejected by radon decay passed through the inner wall and decelerated in the surrounding vacuum, capturing free electrons drifting in from the walls of the outer tube. The neutralized atoms accumulated as a thin gas. After six days Rutherford and Royds compressed the collected gas into the upper end of the apparatus by raising a column of mercury, then struck an electric discharge between the electrodes. The discharge glowed yellow. Rutherford looked at the glow through his spectroscope. D₃ at 587.49 nm stared back, alongside the rest of the helium spectrum: the green D₃′ line at 501.6 nm, the red helium line at 667.8 nm, every characteristic line of neutral helium in the same triplet system Janssen had seen at the limb of the Sun in 1868.

blocks.caption: q/m is the charge-to-mass ratio of the alpha particle, the quantity Rutherford measured in 1903 by magnetic and electric deflection. e is the elementary charge, 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. u is the atomic mass unit, 1.661 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. The 2 in the numerator is the alpha particle's charge in units of e (the bare helium nucleus has charge +2e). The 4 in the denominator is its mass in units of u (the helium-4 nucleus has mass close to 4 u). The numerical value 4.82 × 10⁷ C/kg matched Rutherford's measured q/m for the alpha particle to within his experimental error and identified the alpha as ⁴He²⁺ even before the 1908 Manchester discharge experiment caught the spectrum directly.

blocks.text: Read what the Manchester experiment had done. A spectral line first seen at the limb of the Sun during a total eclipse in 1868 was now reproduced, line for line, by the radioactive decay of a uranium daughter in a glass tube on a Manchester lab bench in 1908. The element identified by Janssen and Lockyer was the same element Ramsay had extracted from cleveite was the same element Rutherford had now collected from the disintegration of radon nuclei. The chain of identification ran from solar chromosphere through terrestrial uranium ore through nuclear decay, and the spectral fingerprint (the D₃ line at 587.49 nm, plus the rest of the triplet) bound the three together. Helium had taken forty years to come fully home: forty years of waiting, two Nobel-level experiments (Ramsay's 1904 Nobel in Chemistry, Rutherford's 1908 Nobel in Chemistry), and a single yellow line each time that said yes, this is the same thing.

finalQuiz.question: Stitch the 1868–1908 helium chain into a single argument. Why did the helium discovery, in retrospect, require three completely independent experimental programs (chromospheric spectroscopy, terrestrial gas chemistry, and radioactivity research), and what was the spectroscopic link that bound them together?
options: Helium was discovered three times because nineteenth-century communication between London, Paris, Stockholm, and Manchester was too poor for the results of one discovery to reach the other laboratories, and the three programs duplicated each other's work. The spectroscopic link is purely coincidental: any element could have been the bridge.

Three independent programs were needed because Lockyer's 1868 chromospheric identification of D₃ at 587.49 nm only ever proved a wavelength, not an atom: an emission line in a hot plasma can be read to a thousandth of a nanometer but does not, by itself, deliver a gas you can weigh, ionize, react, or chemically isolate. Ramsay's 1895 cleveite experiment delivered the gas (a chemically inert sample whose discharge spectrum reproduced D₃ exactly, identifying the chromospheric line as a terrestrial element trapped in uranium-bearing minerals), and Rutherford and Royds's 1908 Manchester experiment delivered the nucleus (a radon decay tube whose collected alpha particles, captured as neutral atoms in vacuum and excited in a discharge, again reproduced the D₃ spectrum). The spectroscopic link is the D₃ line itself: the same wavelength fingerprint appeared in all three settings, and that identity is what bound chromospheric astronomy, mineral chemistry, and nuclear physics into a single story about one element. Each program was necessary because each addressed a different question (what wavelength, what gas, what nucleus) that the others could not answer.

Three independent programs were needed because helium has three distinct stable isotopes (³He, ⁴He, ⁵He), one of which is detected only in the chromosphere, one only in cleveite minerals, and one only in radioactive decay. The discovery program ran in parallel because the isotopes had to be characterized separately.

Three independent programs were needed because the Académie des Sciences refused to accept Lockyer's chromospheric identification, the Royal Society refused to accept Ramsay's terrestrial identification, and only Rutherford's nuclear identification was politically acceptable to both. The spectroscopic link was added retroactively as a unifying narrative.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 0 erases the genuine intellectual structure of the discovery and substitutes a story of communication failure that did not happen; Lockyer and Janssen's 1868 letters reached the Académie within weeks of each other, Ramsay published his cleveite result in the Proceedings of the Royal Society in the same month he ran the experiment, and Rutherford and Royds's 1908 paper cites both. The three programs were independent because they answered different questions, not because they were ignorant of each other. Option 2 invents an isotopic structure to the discovery that did not exist; ³He was not detected until 1939 (Alvarez and Cornog), and ⁵He is not a bound nucleus at all (it has a lifetime of order 10⁻²¹ s and exists only as a resonance). The 1868-1895-1908 story is entirely a ⁴He story; isotopic distinctions came thirty years later. Option 3 invents a political controversy that did not exist; the Académie commemorated Janssen and Lockyer with a medal in 1868, the Royal Society awarded Ramsay the 1904 Nobel in Chemistry for the noble-gas program (including helium), and Rutherford's 1908 Nobel in Chemistry was awarded the same year as the Manchester experiment. The honest answer is option 1. The three programs answered three different questions about the same atom. Chromospheric spectroscopy in 1868 delivered a wavelength (the D₃ line at 587.49 nm, a fingerprint of an element whose photons carry 2.11 eV), but a wavelength does not, by itself, give you a gas to study. Terrestrial gas chemistry in 1895 delivered a chemically inert sample (helium trapped physically in uranium-bearing cleveite, released by acid dissolution, captured in a discharge tube whose spectrum reproduced D₃ exactly) and gave the periodic table a sample to weigh, freeze, and chemically test. Radioactivity research in 1908 delivered the nucleus (the alpha particle, identified by q/m measurement in 1903 and caught spectroscopically in the Manchester radon experiment, ⁴He²⁺ collected from radioactive decay, neutralized in vacuum, excited in a discharge, and again reproducing the D₃ spectrum). The spectroscopic link, in all three cases, was the same D₃ line: same wavelength, same transition (3³D → 2³P in the neutral helium triplet system, understood only after quantum mechanics in the 1920s), same photon energy of 2.11 eV. The identity of the spectrum across three radically different physical settings (a 6000 K solar atmosphere, a room-temperature discharge tube, a radioactive decay sample) is what made the three programs converge into a single story about one element. The wider lesson is that a spectroscopic fingerprint is a transferable signature of identity, valid across chromosphere and laboratory and decay tube alike, and that the unity of helium across the three settings was the first major demonstration of that principle.
